subjects.name: Biology
subjects.description: Introductory biology covering cell biology, genetics, evolution, and ecology.

classes.name: Bio 101
classes.subject: Biology
classes.description: Foundational biology course for first-year students.

examPeriods.name: First Exam
examPeriods.subject: Biology
examPeriods.description: Covers cell structure, cell membrane, DNA, and mitosis.

examPeriods.name: Second Exam
examPeriods.subject: Biology
examPeriods.description: Covers genetics, evolution, photosynthesis, respiration, and ecology.

questions.text: Which organelle is known as the 'powerhouse of the cell' because it produces ATP?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: What is the primary component of the cell membrane?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Which type of cell lacks a membrane-bound nucleus?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: What is the function of ribosomes in a cell?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: During which phase of mitosis do chromosomes align along the cell's equatorial plate?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: What type of membrane transport moves substances from a region of lower concentration to higher concentration, requiring energy?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: What is the role of the Golgi apparatus in a eukaryotic cell?
questions.subject: Biology

choices.text: Package, modify, and ship proteins and lipids

questions.text: Which molecule serves as the primary carrier of genetic information in most organisms?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: What is the name of the process by which a cell's nucleus divides to produce two genetically identical daughter nuclei?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Which of the following is found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: All living cells have a cell wall.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: DNA is a double-stranded molecule shaped like a double helix.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Mitochondria contain their own DNA separate from the cell's nuclear DNA.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: The nucleus of a cell contains RNA but not DNA.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Osmosis is the diffusion of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane from a region of higher water concentration to lower water concentration.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: State the three principles of the Cell Theory.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: 1) All living organisms are made of one or more cells. 2) The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life. 3) All cells arise from pre-existing cells.

questions.text: What are the four nitrogenous bases found in DNA, and which bases pair with each other?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: Adenine (A), Thymine (T), Guanine (G), and Cytosine (C). A pairs with T, and G pairs with C (complementary base pairing).

questions.text: What is the main difference between mitosis and meiosis in terms of the resulting cells?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid daughter cells (used for growth and repair), while meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid cells (gametes for sexual reproduction).

questions.text: Define the term 'selectively permeable' as it applies to the cell membrane.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: Selectively permeable means the cell membrane allows certain substances to pass through while restricting others, regulating the movement of materials into and out of the cell.

questions.text: What is the role of ATP in cellular processes?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the primary energy currency of the cell. It stores and transfers energy for cellular work including active transport, muscle contraction, and biosynthesis reactions.

questions.text: Match each cell organelle to its primary function.
questions.subject: Biology

pairs.right: Houses DNA and controls cell activities
Produces ATP through cellular respiration
Synthesizes proteins
Stores water, nutrients, or waste
Contains digestive enzymes to break down waste

questions.text: Match each phase of mitosis to its key event.
questions.subject: Biology

pairs.right: Chromosomes condense and become visible
Chromosomes align at the cell's equator
Sister chromatids are pulled to opposite poles
Nuclear envelopes reform around each set of chromosomes

questions.text: Match each type of transport to its description.
questions.subject: Biology

pairs.right: Movement of small nonpolar molecules across the membrane without assistance
Transport of molecules via protein channels, no energy required
Movement against the concentration gradient using ATP
Diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane

questions.text: Describe the structure of the cell membrane and explain how its structure relates to its function in regulating what enters and exits the cell.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.rubric: Should include: phospholipid bilayer structure (hydrophilic heads / hydrophobic tails), embedded proteins (channel, carrier, receptor), cholesterol for fluidity. Function should address selective permeability, passive vs. active transport, and the fluid mosaic model.

questions.text: Explain the stages of mitosis and describe why accurate cell division is critical for the health of an organism.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.rubric: Should include: PMAT stages (Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase) with key events, cytokinesis, and why genetic accuracy matters (growth, repair, cancer risk if errors occur).

questions.text: What is a gene?
questions.subject: Biology

choices.text: A strand of RNA that codes for a protein
A segment of DNA that codes for a specific protein or trait

questions.text: In a monohybrid cross between two heterozygous parents (Aa × Aa), what is the expected phenotypic ratio of the offspring?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Who is known as the 'Father of Genetics' for his work with pea plants?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes natural selection?
questions.subject: Biology

choices.text: Organisms change their traits on purpose to survive
Organisms with favorable traits survive and reproduce more successfully
All members of a population are equally likely to reproduce
Traits are acquired during an organism's lifetime and passed to offspring

questions.text: What is the overall chemical equation for photosynthesis?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Where in the cell does photosynthesis take place?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: What is the term for an organism that obtains energy by consuming other organisms?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Which of the following describes the genotype of an organism?
questions.subject: Biology

choices.text: The physical appearance of an organism
The allele combination an organism possesses for a trait
The environment in which an organism lives
The number of chromosomes in a cell

questions.text: What is the role of decomposers in an ecosystem?
questions.subject: Biology

choices.text: Break down dead organic matter and recycle nutrients

questions.text: Which process produces the most ATP per glucose molecule?
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: A dominant allele will always be expressed over a recessive allele when both are present in a heterozygous individual.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: All mutations are harmful to an organism.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Chloroplasts are found only in plant cells and algae, not in animal cells.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Evolution occurs in individual organisms over the course of their lifetime.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: In a food web, energy is transferred with 100% efficiency from one trophic level to the next.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Explain Mendel's Law of Segregation in your own words.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: Each organism has two alleles for each trait, and these alleles separate (segregate) during the formation of gametes so that each gamete receives only one allele. When gametes combine during fertilization, the offspring inherits one allele from each parent.

questions.text: What is the difference between dominant and recessive alleles? Give an example of each.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype whenever it is present (e.g., brown eye color). A recessive allele is only expressed in the phenotype when an organism has two copies of it (e.g., blue eye color, expressed only in homozygous recessives).

questions.text: Describe the two main stages of photosynthesis and where each occurs within the chloroplast.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: 1) Light-dependent reactions occur in the thylakoid membranes; they use light energy to split water, produce ATP and NADPH, and release oxygen. 2) The Calvin Cycle (light-independent reactions) occurs in the stroma; it uses ATP and NADPH to fix CO2 into glucose.

questions.text: What is natural selection? Provide one example from nature.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: Natural selection is the process by which organisms with traits better suited to their environment tend to survive and reproduce more, passing those traits to offspring. Example: Peppered moths — dark-colored moths had higher survival rates during the Industrial Revolution when trees were darkened by soot.

questions.text: What is the difference between a food chain and a food web?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.answer: A food chain shows a single linear pathway of energy transfer from producers to consumers. A food web shows the complex, interconnected network of all food chains in an ecosystem, depicting multiple feeding relationships among species.

questions.text: Match each genetics term to its correct definition.
questions.subject: Biology

pairs.right: The genetic makeup (allele combination) of an organism
The observable physical traits of an organism
Having two identical alleles for a trait (e.g., AA or aa)
Having two different alleles for a trait (e.g., Aa)
A variant form of a gene

questions.text: Match each ecological term to its correct description.
questions.subject: Biology

pairs.right: All members of one species in a given area
All the populations of different species in an area
A community plus its abiotic (non-living) environment
A large geographical area defined by climate and dominant vegetation

questions.text: Match each stage of cellular respiration to its primary location in the cell.
questions.subject: Biology

pairs.right: Cytoplasm
Mitochondrial matrix
Inner mitochondrial membrane
Cytoplasm (anaerobic conditions)

questions.text: Explain the central dogma of molecular biology (DNA → RNA → Protein). Describe each step and explain why this process is fundamental to all life.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.rubric: Should include: Transcription (DNA → mRNA in nucleus), Translation (mRNA → protein at ribosomes), role of tRNA and codons, and why protein synthesis drives all cellular functions. Should mention that mutations in DNA affect the protein produced.

questions.text: Describe the flow of energy through an ecosystem, starting from the sun. Include producers, consumers, decomposers, and explain what happens to energy at each trophic level.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.rubric: Should include: Producers capture solar energy via photosynthesis; primary consumers eat producers; secondary/tertiary consumers follow; ~10% energy rule (only 10% transferred to next level, rest lost as heat); decomposers recycle nutrients; energy flows one-way while matter is cycled.
